source.title: directed evolution of a cyclodipeptide synthase with new activities via label fr

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Directed evolution of enzymes is limited by the lack of high-throughput screening (HTS) assays for new catalytic activities. Mass spectrometry (MS) is label-free and suitable for untargeted discovery but traditionally slow. The goal is to develop an automated, label-free MS screening workflow for directed evolution of new enzymatic activities, demonstrated by engineering a cyclodipeptide synthase (CDPS) to produce new diketopiperazine (DKP) products.

section_gists.heading: Directed evolution of a cyclodipeptide synthase with new activities via label-free mass spectrometric screening†
section_gists.gist: The paper presents an automated workflow for directed evolution using label-free MALDI-ToF MS screening. It applies this to engineer AlbC, a CDPS, to produce new DKP products. Site-saturation mutagenesis (SSM) and error-prone PCR (epPCR) libraries were created in E. coli, cultured, and supernatants processed robotically for MS analysis at 5 s per sample. Custom algorithms analyzed 108 theoretical m/z values for 190 possible DKPs. An F186L mutant was isolated that produces cyclo(L-Phe-L-Val), undetectable in wild-type. The workflow extends previous agar colony MS screening to liquid cultures with automated sample preparation to overcome ion suppression.
process_signals: Previous MS screening was on agar colonies; this work moves to liquid cultures for better uniformity.
Liquid culture media cause ion suppression, requiring automated sample preparation (liquid-liquid extraction, solid-phase extraction).
Untargeted MS screening requires careful standardization to minimize noise and detect weak new product signals.
CDPSs are recalcitrant to rational engineering; large-scale screening of CDPS variants not previously reported due to lack of HTS assays.
The F186L mutant was isolated from a library, showing a new product not seen in wild-type.
methods: Site-saturation mutagenesis (SSM) and error-prone PCR (epPCR) for library creation
Automated cultivation in microplates on an integrated work cell
Robotic sample preparation for MALDI-ToF MS (liquid-liquid extraction, solid-phase extraction)
MALDI-ToF MS analysis at 5 s per sample
Custom computational algorithms for multivariant analysis of 108 theoretical m/z values of 190 possible DKP molecules (mass window 115–373 Da)
metrics: Number of theoretical m/z values monitored: 108
failures_or_limits: Liquid culture media interfere with MALDI matrix crystallization and cause ion suppression, necessitating extra sample preparation.
CDPSs are recalcitrant to rational engineering; no prior large-scale screening reported.
Only one mutant (F186L) reported with new activity; other variants not detailed.
Untargeted MS screening requires careful optimization to detect weak signals.

section_gists.heading: Data availability
section_gists.gist: Datasets supporting the article are available in the ESI.

section_gists.heading: Author contributions
section_gists.gist: T.S., J.Z., and S.Y.Z. conceived the project and experimental design. J.Z. and S.Y.Z. conducted wet experimental work. S.Y.Z., S.F., and Z.Y.Y. performed molecular docking and dynamic simulation. W.H.X. assisted with sample preparation for MALDI-ToF MS. S.Y.Z., J.Z., S.F., and T.S. prepared the manuscript.
process_signals: Molecular docking and dynamic simulation were performed, indicating computational guidance in library design or analysis.
Wet lab and computational work were done by overlapping teams.

section_gists.heading: Acknowledgements
section_gists.gist: Funding from National Key R&D Program, NSFC, and postdoctoral grant. Infrastructure support from Shenzhen Synthetic Biology Infrastructure for robotic workflow development.
process_signals: Robotic workflow development was supported by a synthetic biology infrastructure, indicating reliance on biofoundry capabilities.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research aimed to overcome the HTS bottleneck for new enzymatic activities by leveraging label-free MS in an automated biofoundry setting.
CDPS AlbC was chosen as a model system because it produces pharmacologically relevant DKPs and is known to be difficult to engineer rationally.
The hypothesis was that untargeted MS screening could detect weak new product signals from mutant libraries, enabling isolation of variants with altered substrate specificity.
Computational docking and dynamics were likely used to guide mutagenesis (e.g., targeting active site residues) or to rationalize the F186L mutant's new activity.
The workflow was designed to be generalizable to other enzymes.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What specific residues were targeted in SSM libraries and why?
How were the 108 theoretical m/z values selected?
What were the results of the epPCR library screening?
Were there other hits besides F186L?
What were the kinetic parameters and product characterization of the F186L mutant?
How did molecular docking and dynamics inform library design or explain the F186L mutation?
What were the limitations in throughput, substrate scope, or scalability?
Were there any negative results or failed attempts in the evolution process?

events.summary: Assay development for high-throughput mutant screening remains challenging, particularly for new catalytic activities.
CDPSs are recalcitrant targets for rational engineering, and large-scale screening of CDPS variants has not been reported due to lack of HTS assays.
Develop an automated workflow for directed evolution of new enzymatic activities via high-throughput library creation and label-free MS screening.
Choose AlbC CDPS as a proof-of-concept target to engineer new DKP products.
Create SSM and epPCR libraries of AlbC mutants in E. coli, automatically cultivate, and robotically process for MALDI-ToF MS at 5 s per sample.
Process mass spectral data via custom algorithms performing multivariant analysis of 108 theoretical m/z values of 190 possible DKP molecules.
Validate workflow by detecting known CDP products from WT AlbC-expressing E. coli, consistent with literature.
No new CDP molecules were observed from the 14 SSM library mutants targeting binding pocket residues.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers introduction, workflow design, validation, and initial SSM library screening results, including a failure to find new products.

events.summary: No new CDP molecules were produced from the 14 SSM library mutants.
epPCR libraries with random mutations throughout the whole protein sequence were created to overcome SSM limitations.
methods_or_tools: Agilent GeneMorph II Random Mutagenesis Kit
numbers_or_metrics: on average 2 nucleotides introduced per gene variant

events.summary: Around 4500 independent clones were screened using the automated MS workflow.
Peak intensities at theoretical m/z values were analyzed by one-way ANOVA with Tukey's multiple comparison test, revealing a new peak at m/z 247 corresponding to cFV.
Computational modeling reveals that F186L mutation abolishes π-π stacking, increases binding cavity volume, and alters substrate binding energy.
numbers_or_metrics: binding cavity volume increased from 197 Å³ to 288 Å³

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains key experimental results: failure of SSM libraries, shift to epPCR, screening of ~4500 clones, identification and validation of F186L mutant producing cFV, and computational analysis explaining the mechanism.

evidence_bank.quote: assay development for high-throughput mutant screening remains challenging, particularly for new catalytic activities.
CDPSs are recognized as recalcitrant targets for rational engineering.
large-scale screening of CDPS variants has not been reported, possibly due to the lack of applicable HTS assays.
Here we report an automated workflow for directed evolution of new enzymatic activities via high-throughput library creation and label-free MS screening.
For a proof of concept, we chose to engineer a cyclodipeptide synthase (CDPS) that synthesizes diketopiperazine (DKP) compounds with therapeutic potential.
site-saturation mutagenesis (SSM) and error-prone PCR (epPCR) libraries expressing CDPS mutants were automatically created and cultivated on an integrated work cell.
Culture supernatants were then robotically processed for matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight (MALDI-ToF) MS analysis at a rate of 5 s per sample.

The resulting mass spectral data were processed via custom computational algorithms, which performed a multivariant analysis of 108 theoretical mass-to-charge (m/z) values of 190 possible DKP molecules within a mass window of 115–373 Da.
These results validated our workflow to detect CDP products from microplate cultures of AlbC-expressing E. coli using MALDI-ToF MS.
Unfortunately, we did not observe any new CDP molecules produced from the 14 SSM library mutants.
Unfortunately, we did not observe any new CDP molecules produced from the 14 SSM library mutants.
Therefore, we further turned to profile epPCR libraries that contain random mutations throughout the whole protein sequence.

Under optimized conditions using the Agilent GeneMorph II Random Mutagenesis Kit, on average 2 nucleotides were introduced per gene variant so that most epPCR library members contain no more than one amino acid change.
In total around 4500 independent clones were screened using the above workflow.
The peak intensities at the theoretical m/z values of predicted cyclodipeptide ions in each MALDI mass spectrum were analyzed by the one-way ANOVA with Tukey's multiple comparison test.

Notably, by creating and screening $\sim$ 4500 epPCR library members within a week using unlabeled MS on an integrated, robotic workcell, an AlbC mutant producing a new cyclopeptide product was identified, revealing a previously unknown residue (F186) that exerts a substantial impact on substrate specificity.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Directed evolution of enzymes is limited by the lack of high-throughput screening (HTS) assays for new catalytic activities. Mass spectrometry (MS) is label-free and suitable for untargeted discovery but traditionally slow. The goal is to develop an automated, label-free MS screening workflow for directed evolution of new enzymatic activities, demonstrated by engineering a cyclodipeptide synthase (CDPS) to produce new diketopiperazine (DKP) products.

canonical_events.summary: Assay development for high-throughput mutant screening remains challenging, particularly for new catalytic activities.
CDPSs are recalcitrant targets for rational engineering, and large-scale screening of CDPS variants has not been reported due to lack of HTS assays.
Develop an automated workflow for directed evolution of new enzymatic activities via high-throughput library creation and label-free MS screening.
Choose AlbC CDPS as a proof-of-concept target to engineer new DKP products.
Create SSM and epPCR libraries of AlbC mutants in E. coli, automatically cultivate, and robotically process for MALDI-ToF MS at 5 s per sample.
Process mass spectral data via custom algorithms performing multivariant analysis of 108 theoretical m/z values of 190 possible DKP molecules (mass window 115–373 Da).
Validate workflow by detecting known CDP products from WT AlbC-expressing E. coli, consistent with literature.
No new CDP molecules were observed from the 14 SSM library mutants targeting binding pocket residues.
Create epPCR libraries with random mutations throughout the whole protein sequence to overcome SSM limitations (on average 2 nucleotides introduced per gene variant).
key_numbers: on average 2 nucleotides introduced per gene variant

canonical_events.summary: Screen around 4500 independent epPCR clones using the automated MS workflow.
Analyze peak intensities at theoretical m/z values by one-way ANOVA with Tukey's multiple comparison test, revealing a new peak at m/z 247 corresponding to cFV.
Isolate F186L CDPS mutant producing cyclo(L-Phe-L-Val) (cFV), undetectable in wild-type, and confirm by LC-MS/MS and Sanger sequencing.
Computational modeling reveals that F186L mutation abolishes π-π stacking, increases binding cavity volume from 197 Å³ to 288 Å³, and alters substrate binding energy (F186L-cFV: -5.37 kcal/mol).
key_numbers: binding cavity volume increased from 197 Å³ to 288 Å³

canonical_events.summary: Only one mutant (F186L) reported with new activity; other variants not detailed; untargeted MS screening requires careful optimization to detect weak signals.

edges.reason: General HTS challenge motivates specific problem with CDPSs.
Lack of HTS for CDPSs motivates development of automated MS workflow.
Workflow design is refined by choosing AlbC as proof-of-concept.
Target selection enables library creation and screening.
MS data generation enables computational analysis.
Analysis method enables validation with WT.
After validation, SSM library screening yields no new products.
Failure of SSM motivates epPCR library design.
epPCR library design enables large-scale screening.
Screening data enables statistical analysis revealing new peak.
Detection of new peak leads to isolation and validation of F186L mutant.
Isolation of mutant enables computational modeling to explain mechanism.
After mechanistic insights, limitations of the study are noted.

must_cover_points: Problem of HTS for new activities
CDPS recalcitrance and lack of screening
Isolation and validation of F186L mutant
